tagged_story_for_tts:
Typos in commands.<[silence:600ms]> Logic that forgets edge cases.<[silence:600ms]>

The phrase "bug" comes from 1947, when a moth got trapped in Harvard’s Mark II computer, jamming its relays—literally a bug in the machine.<[silence:600ms]> But most errors aren’t insects;<[silence:200ms]> they’re invisible missteps, like writing "if temperature > 100 Celsius" but forgetting to handle "exactly 100".<[silence:600ms]>

Errors sneak in three ways:<[silence:200ms]>
1.<[silence:600ms]> Assumptions – A programmer assumes users will enter numbers, not letters.<[silence:600ms]> Then someone types "apple", and the system crashes.<[silence:600ms]>
2.<[silence:600ms]> Shortcuts – Copy-pasting code saves time, but if the original had a hidden flaw (e.g., "check for emails with @" but misses '+' symbols), the flaw spreads.<[silence:600ms]>
3.<[silence:600ms]> Blind spots – A traffic light system works until two cars arrive at the same millisecond—a scenario no one tested.<[silence:600ms]>

Real-world example:<[silence:200ms]> In 1996, the Ariane 5 rocket exploded 37 seconds after launch because engineers reused code from Ariane 4, but the new rocket’s faster speed triggered a numeric overflow (a number too big for its container).<[silence:600ms]> The fix?<[silence:200ms]> One extra line of error-checking.<[silence:600ms]>

Three related subjects are code testing tricks, how computers misread time, and famous software disasters.<[silence:600ms]>
clean_story:
Typos in commands. Logic that forgets edge cases.

The phrase "bug" comes from 1947, when a moth got trapped in Harvard’s Mark II computer, jamming its relays—literally a bug in the machine. But most errors aren’t insects; they’re invisible missteps, like writing "if temperature > 100 Celsius" but forgetting to handle "exactly 100".

Errors sneak in three ways:
1. Assumptions – A programmer assumes users will enter numbers, not letters. Then someone types "apple", and the system crashes.
2. Shortcuts – Copy-pasting code saves time, but if the original had a hidden flaw (e.g., "check for emails with @" but misses '+' symbols), the flaw spreads.
3. Blind spots – A traffic light system works until two cars arrive at the same millisecond—a scenario no one tested.

Real-world example: In 1996, the Ariane 5 rocket exploded 37 seconds after launch because engineers reused code from Ariane 4, but the new rocket’s faster speed triggered a numeric overflow (a number too big for its container). The fix? One extra line of error-checking.

Three related subjects are code testing tricks, how computers misread time, and famous software disasters.
